subject: LU-3336 lfsck: use rbtree to record OST-object accessing

values.-1: I would prefer that you didn't submit this
values.+1: Looks good to me, but someone else must approve

Build Successful

Patch Set 1:

Tests received by maloo, run on el6/x86_64: (https://maloo.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/9a936b82-25e8-11e3-8f1c-52540035b04c).  Ran 2 tests.  1 tests failed: lustre-initialization-1.

Patch Set 1: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

I don't understand why there need to be two bitmaps?  If the first pass marks the bits for all existing objects, then the second pass should clear the bit for all accessed objects.  This would reduce the memory usage over time instead of increase it.  At the end of the traversal, the bitmap will only contain FIDs that have not been accessed, and it can just be listed directly instead of needing even more processing to compare the bitmaps.

Patch Set 1: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

is there really a big need to track objects accessed by OFD ? we're going to touch them by LFSCK crawling over all the objects any way?

also notice that OUT talks to OSD directly.

Patch Set 2: Fails

Tests received by maloo, run on CentOS release 6.4/x86_64: (https://maloo.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/08376730-2c40-11e3-aeed-52540035b04c).  Ran 24 tests.  5 tests failed: sanity-hsm, sanity-lfsck, conf-sanity, sanityn, sanity.

Patch Set 2: I would prefer that you didn't submit this

(15 inline comments)

I don't think you read my previous comment on patch #1, or at least there was no response to it and the patch hasn't been updated.

I don't understand why there need to be two bitmaps in memory (both the "known-bitmap" and the "accessed-bitmap").  Having a single "known-bitmap" that is generated from OST-driven object iteration should be enough, since any object accessed during scanning that would be added to the "accessed-bitmap" should just be removed from the "known-bitmap" instead of doubling the memory needed to track both bitmaps, and then doing additional work to compare the bitmaps at the end.

As the MDS-driven object iteration progresses the number of entries in the "known-bitmap" shrinks until it is empty (in the normal case), or has only orphan OST objects at the end of the MDS iteration.  In either case, the amount of memory allocated for the bitmap should be shrinking as the MDT-driven iteration continues instead of growing.

The current implementation doesn't follow the HLD.

Patch Set 13: Code-Review+1

those hooks in OFD/OUT look quite tricky to me. this can work, but makes OUT/OFD required things for LFSCK, which at some can be hard to keep in some configurations (like LFSCK running on a node with few local devices which we can access directly). personally I'd think pushing compressed arrays of FIDs from the master to the slaves should be OK, but I do understand the argument it's still extra-traffic ...

Patch Set 13:

Introducing the hook is really because no better way. Based on our current stack layers, in spite of LFSCK or OFD or TGT module to handle the OST-objects bitmap are all trouble things.

Your idea of push FID arrays from master to slave may works, but it needs more API and on-wrie protocol changes, and as you mentioned the traffic. From both efficiency and logic complexity view, it looks not so good...

Patch Set 13:

I don't think any API changes would be needed - OSD API can be used to transfer information either in form of key/value (index) or just blobs (writes) which we do with OUT anyway to implement other features. it's less efficient because we have to transfer over a network, but the question is whether we really need that level of efficiency. uncompressed data would be like 1,5GB for 100M objects, which is quite amount, but these days we put dozens GBs *every* second easily. so 1,5GB over minutes (or likely hours) won't be really visible, IMO.

Patch Set 13:

That means both the MDT and the OST needs to maintain the bitmap, and on the MDT side, which layer will do that? still in LFSCK? if yes, how it can handle the new-created OST-objects during the LFSCK scanning? Seems OSP has to know LFSCK related things, and there may be still some hack code to be done.

Patch Set 13:

Rosso Autotest has started the test process

Patch Set 13:

Rosso Autotest has started the test process

Patch Set 13:

Tests received by maloo, run on CentOS release 6.4/x86_64: (https://maloo.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/58f5d9bc-7881-11e3-9a46-52540035b04c).  Ran 6 tests.  1 tests failed: sanity.

Patch Set 13:

hmm, why would that mean so? to me it means that LFSCK on MDT collects arrays(blobs) of FIDs found referenced and then push them to the slave LFSCK. and then the slave uses that information locally - to build rbtree from that, for example. from my point of view, the less LFSCK bits in the modules like OSP/OFD, the better. in a perfect case, LFSCK is self-contained and just uses some very generic mechanisms like OSD API, LDLM, configuration, ptlrpc.

Patch Set 13:

Rosso Autotest has started the test process

Patch Set 13:

Using your way just moves related work from OFD to OSP, because we need to record the OST-objects those are new-created or pre-created during the LFSCK scanning, the LFSCK on the MDT cannot detect/collect them by itself.

Patch Set 13:

Tests received by maloo, run on CentOS release 6.4/x86_64: (https://maloo.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/88056bca-78ca-11e3-9f31-52540035b04c).  Ran 19 tests.  4 tests failed: sanity-hsm, ost-pools, sanityn, sanity.

Patch Set 13:

Tests received by maloo, run on CentOS release 6.4/x86_64: (https://maloo.whamcloud.com/test_sessions/fb7fd8a8-7927-11e3-8433-52540035b04c).  Ran 19 tests.  4 tests failed: sanity-lfsck, ost-pools, sanityn, sanity.

Patch Set 13:

I don't understand the latest statement. my suggestion was about that hacky hook to track accessed objects in OFD/OSP.

Patch Set 13:

Your way can avoid using hack hook on OFD for recording the accessed OST-objects, but it will cause similar hack hook to be used on MDT side. For example, during the LFSCK scanning, the MDT creates some new files, these files have not been scanned by the LFSCK because the iteration position has already exceeded them. Then after the first cycle scanning, as your way, the LFSCK push the FIDs array (its scanned) to OST for finding out orphans. At that time, those new created files will be regraded as orphans. So when those files are created, the MDT needs some mechanism to record them, so similar hack hook needed by MDT/OSP?

Patch Set 13:

I'd think something like ctime should be enough to skip objects created since LFSCK start.

Patch Set 13:

Firstly, ctime is not accurate enough, there will be race, and not easy to control the race.

Secondly, there will be pre-created OST-objects. From the OST view, they have already been used, but from the MDT view, those are non-used OST-objects, and the LFSCK on the MDT cannot record them unless some hook in OSP.

Patch Set 13:

from logical point of view it doesn't matter where access information comes from. e.g. OUT has nothing to do with OFD, but we trust that information.

Patch Set 13:

from logical point of view it doesn't matter where access information comes from. e.g. OUT has nothing to do with OFD, but we trust that information.

Patch Set 13:

The LFSCK cannot know some of the accessing information by itself, so there must be some other modules to collect related accessing information, then tell LFSCK via some hook. In my patch, they are OFD and OUT, and your way will be OSP/OUT. Anyway, the hook is necessary, the difference is where the hook is.

Patch Set 13:

sorry, I don't think you understand what i'm saying. there is no need for LFSCK to catch orphans right away and we agreed on this during design discussion. then in the ideal case LFSCK should be relying on on-disk information only (IMHO). and problems like "just created objects" can be solved by simple means like ctime or version or even "skip objects since LASTID-XX".

Patch Set 12:

(6 comments)

Patch Set 15: Code-Review+1

probably few minor improvements are possible, see the comments I just pushed in the old patch.

I'd like to put for a record here that strictly speaking we should not treat any access to object as a sign of reference. Imagine malicious client (evicted, broken, injected) keep accessing an object which isn't referenced any more. of course, this isn't a frequent (or even very practical) case, but still it's only access from LFSCK who can be a proved reference?

Patch Set 15:

Alex, I'm not so worried about a client keeping an orphan object around. The next time LFSCK runs it will find the orphan, or the next time, etc since clients do not live forever.

Patch Set 15:

hmm, what would prevent broken client to keep touching an object for days or weeks? I don't think this is very practical case (at least now), but may happen in theory?

Patch Set 15:

I think that we can use capability to prevent bad client to keep touching an orphan object for days/weeks. But unfortunately, current capability has some security holes and bad performance, so it is not used in real product environment.

Patch Set 12:

(6 comments)

parents.subject: LU-3594 lfsck: repair inconsistent owner and multiple referenced cases

commit.subject: LU-3336 lfsck: use rbtree to record OST-object accessing
commit.message:
LU-3336 lfsck: use rbtree to record OST-object accessing

To find out orphan OST-objects, the LFSCK on OST side maintains
two bitmaps in RAM for the OST-object accessed during the LFSCK.
After the first cycle system scanning, the LFSCK got the bitmap
for the known OST-objects, and got another bitmap for which OST
objects have been referenced by MDT-objects. Then the LFSCK can
know which OST-objects are not referenced by any MDT-object via
comparing the two bitmaps.

Above two bitmaps are organized via a single rbtree. The rbtree
is maintained by LFSCK on the OST side. For every LFSCK scanned
OST-object, it will be recorded in the known-bitmap, for every
OST-object accessed by any RPC during the scanning, it will be
recoreded in the accessed-bitmap.

Signed-off-by: Fan Yong <fan.yong@intel.com>
Change-Id: If399584a8617e7c368e48922a3582294ac98d5f4
Reviewed-on: http://review.whamcloud.com/7743
